cards.title: WA — the topic marker
cards.rule: WA marks the topic you are talking ABOUT. Written 'ha' but said 'wa' — the only common spelling/sound mismatch in Japanese. Use it to introduce or highlight the thing your sentence is about.
cards.plain: Put WA right after the thing you're saying something about.

examples.gloss: Toilet-wa where is? — As for the toilet, where is it?
This-wa what is it? — What is this?
Recommendation-wa what is it? — What do you recommend?

cards.contrast: WA vs GA: WA is for the known topic ('as for X'). GA introduces, emphasizes, or marks the subject of an action. 'Eigo ga hanasemasu ka' (English-is-can-speak) emphasizes the language; 'Eigo wa hanasemasu ka' (English-as-for-can-speak) would mean 'as for English, can you speak it?'. Same words, different focus.

cards.title: O — the direct object
cards.rule: O marks the object of an action — the thing you do something to. It's always silent-spelled 'o' even when reading kana aloud (the kana is written を but said 'o').
cards.plain: Put O after the thing you want, see, drink, buy, eat.

examples.gloss: Menu-o please — 'the menu, please'.
This-o please — 'this, please'.
Water-o please — 'water, please'.

cards.contrast: Don't confuse the particle O with plain 'o' elsewhere. As a particle, it's always silent-written. As part of a word like 'tokyo', it's pronounced 'oh' or 'o' depending on context. The script usually makes it clear.

cards.title: GA — the subject / existence
cards.rule: GA marks the doer of an action, or the thing that exists (in arimasu / imasu — 'there is'). When something new is being introduced, GA is the natural fit.
cards.plain: Use GA when something is being introduced or emphasized. Use WA when you're already on the topic.

examples.gloss: Japanese-ga can't-speak — As for me being able to speak Japanese (no).
Doesn't understand — 'I (implicit subject) don't understand' uses WA, but adding the noun flips to GA.
Small change-ga not exists — 'I don't have small change'.

cards.contrast: Most of the time the difference is natural — subject for new information, topic for ongoing conversation. Don't overthink it; you'll be right most of the time.

cards.title: NI — destination, time, location of existence
cards.rule: NI has three main uses: (1) destination/direction (go TO a place), (2) a specific point in time (at 5 o'clock), and (3) the place where something exists (the cup is ON the table).
cards.plain: Use NI for 'to' places, 'at' times, and 'in/on' locations of existence.

examples.gloss: Stop AT a station — '[this] stops at ~.'
AT 5 o'clock — 'at ~ o'clock'.
TO the onsen — 'I want to go in the onsen'.

cards.contrast: For destination, NI and E (へ) overlap. NI = 'arrival point' (you want to land somewhere specific); E = 'direction' (you're heading that way). Use E with motion verbs (iku = go), NI with verbs of arrival or existence.

cards.title: DE — means & location of action
cards.rule: DE marks the instrument/manner 'with / by' (pay by cash) or the place where an action happens (eat AT a restaurant). It's the 'how/where-does-the-action-happen' particle.
cards.plain: Use DE for the tool/method (cash, card) or the action's location.

examples.gloss: By card — 'can I pay by card?'.
By cash — 'I'll pay cash'.
By Suica — 'can I pay with Suica?'.

cards.contrast: DE = where the action happens. NI = where something exists. You 'meet DE the station' (action), but a cafe is NI the station (it exists there).

cards.title: E — direction
cards.rule: E marks direction, written へ but said 'eh'. The kana へ belongs to the 'ha-hi-fu-he-ho' row but as a particle is read 'e'.
cards.plain: Use E with motion verbs — go TOWARD a place.

examples.gloss: TO this address — point on phone, taxi driver takes you there.
TO  ~ — 'I want to go to ~'.

cards.contrast: E for direction; NI for the destination itself. Use E for 'head toward Tokyo Station', use NI when you mean 'arrive at / exist at Tokyo Station'. Both work for taxis; pick one and stay consistent.

cards.title: KARA (from) and MADE (until/until you reach)
cards.rule: KARA marks a starting point — 'from ~'. MADE marks a limit — 'up to ~ / until ~'. Often paired: 'from A to B'.
cards.plain: Use KARA for 'since/from'. Use MADE for 'until/up to (place or time)'.

examples.gloss: From here is it far? — distance question from KARA.
Up to ~ how much — taxi fare estimate up to a destination.

cards.title: TO — and / with
cards.rule: TO links nouns (A and B). Also 'together with' (someone). Always read 'to' even when spelled とう.
cards.plain: Drop TO between nouns to list them.

examples.gloss: Meat and fish — 'meat and fish (both) I can't eat'.
Two separate bills — 'betsu-betsu' includes 'and' implicit in 'each, separately'.

cards.title: NO — of / possessive / linking nouns
cards.rule: NO links nouns to other nouns: 'a NO b' = 'b's a' or 'a of b' (Japanese is flexible about direction).
cards.plain: Use NO when a noun describes or modifies another noun.

examples.gloss: Shinkansen-no reserved-seat — 'reserved seat on the shinkansen'.
English-no menu — 'an English menu'.
Smith-to-iu name — 'a name called Smith'.

cards.title: DESU and MASU — the polite layer
cards.rule: Every phrase in this app uses -DESU (polite 'is') or -MASU (polite verbs). Together they make almost anything polite. They are the only forms tourists need for almost every situation.
cards.plain: Use -DESU/-MASU with strangers, in shops, restaurants, hotels. Use plain forms (NO da, NO masu endings) only with close friends or children.

examples.gloss: Daijoubu desu — the polite 'I'm fine'. Casually you'd say 'daijoubu'.
Wakarimasen — polite. Casual: 'wakaranai'.

cards.title: Two request engines: KUDASAI and ONEGAI SHIMASU
cards.rule: KUDASAI = 'please (give me / do)' — direct, common. ONEGAI SHIMASU = 'please (I request)' — softer and broader. Both pair naturally after any verb.
cards.plain: Attach either to the end of a verb: 'mizu o KUDASAI' (water please), 'menyuu o ONEGAI SHIMASU' (menu please).

examples.gloss: Water-O kudasai — direct request.
Menu-O onegai shimasu — softer/broader request.

cards.title: KA — yes/no questions
cards.rule: Add KA to the end of a sentence and raise your intonation. That's a yes/no question. No verb inversion, no 'do'.
cards.plain: Append KA to a declarative statement.

examples.gloss: Do you speak English? → 'English-can-speak KA?'
Can I use a card? → 'card-can-use KA?'
